one_line_claim: MEME argues that LLM-agent memory benchmarks miss multi-entity evolving dependency reasoning, and shows that six practical memory systems mostly fail Cascade and Absence tasks.
what_they_tested: The authors build 100 synthetic episodes across Personal Life and Software Project graphs, with about 35K tokens per episode and 694 questions over Exact Recall, Aggregation, Tracking, Deletion, Cascade, and Absence. They evaluate BM25, text-embedding-3-small retrieval, Mem0, Graphiti, MD-flat, Karpathy Wiki, plus in-context and gold-facts ceilings, then run prompt, top-k, noise, answer-LLM, and internal-LLM interventions.
strongest_point: The best part is the diagnostic framing: the paper does not just report low scores, it traces failures through encoding, maintenance, retrieval, and answering, and uses trivial-pass filtering so Absence and Deletion are not accidentally rewarded when a system never remembered the original fact.
weakest_point: The benchmark is highly controlled and synthetic: two author-crafted DAGs, LLM-generated conversations, explicit dependency wording, fictitious values, and many ablations on only 10-40 episode subsets. That makes the failure mode plausible and useful, but not yet a broad claim about all deployed agent memory.
missing_baselines: A task-aware deterministic state-store or graph-rule engine that explicitly propagates dependencies, as an upper-bound memory architecture rather than only an LLM-memory comparison.
A full-transcript long-context baseline with the strongest evaluated answer models, not just gpt-4.1-mini and Sonnet 4.6 on the 32K-filler in-context setting.

A recency-aware or hybrid retrieval baseline that deliberately retrieves both the dependent fact/rule and recent upstream changes, since the main failure often concerns stale values outranking change events.
A MemGPT/Letta-style working-plus-archival memory baseline, since the related work names that paradigm but the experiments mainly use Mem0, Graphiti, raw retrieval, and file agents.
Production-tuned configurations for Graphiti and Mem0, including retrieval depth, reranking, temporal filters, and custom extraction prompts, instead of relying mostly on defaults for the main table.
missing_ablations: Implicit, conversational, or partially stated dependency rules; the paper admits the current conditional phrasing is explicit and best-case for memory systems.
More domains and independently authored graphs, because the main dataset uses two hand-crafted author graphs reused across all episodes.
Human-authored or real user sessions versus LLM self-chat, to separate memory-system failure from artifacts of synthetic dialogue generation.
Graph depth, number of dependents, and number of change/delete events per episode, which would show whether the collapse is caused by multi-hop reasoning, entity count, or temporal update density.
Full-size repeated-run stability for all stochastic systems and interventions; the main stability analysis is only a 10-episode subset.
Judge sensitivity beyond 144 annotated samples, especially for Absence and Deletion where wording like uncertainty, no longer available, and old-value leakage can change pass/fail.
red_flags: The headline that current memory systems collapse is directionally supported, but the evidence is limited to six systems, mostly default configurations, and one benchmark design.
Graphiti's near-zero Exact Recall is partly explained by a paraphrasing instruction in its extraction prompt, so overall averages mix genuine memory failure with a known mismatch to a verbatim task.
Several intervention claims are based on small subsets: prompt optimization uses a 10-episode held-out test, top-k and noise use 40 episodes, and internal-LLM swaps use 20 episodes.

The synthetic setup may overestimate real-world solvability in one way and underestimate it in another: explicit conditionals help systems, but fictitious names and template-like dependencies may not match natural user behavior.

The cost story is useful but incomplete for deployment, because it counts token/API cost while leaving latency, storage, orchestration overhead, and non-LLM infrastructure mostly outside the analysis.

The benchmark's gold answers for Cascade and Absence are generated from the authors' DAG rules, so any mismatch between natural pragmatic interpretation and the DAG semantics could be scored as model failure.
positive_signals: The taxonomy is clean and podcast-friendly: entity scope crossed with temporal dynamics naturally isolates why static recall is easier than evolving multi-entity state.
The dataset tries to prevent easy contamination by using fictitious entity values and computes Cascade/Absence gold answers from a DAG rather than from model judgment.
The evaluation includes three memory paradigms: raw retrieval, LLM-processed memory, and file-based agents, which is a stronger comparison than a single RAG baseline.
Trivial-pass filtering for Cascade, Absence, and Deletion is a serious methodological choice because it excludes systems that only answer uncertainty after failing to encode the before-state.
The paper provides per-stage traces and targeted ablations, making the result more actionable than a leaderboard-style benchmark.
The limitations section is unusually direct about hand-crafted graphs, LLM-generated dialogue, 100 episodes, subset ablations, explicit conditional wording, and English-only data.
verdict: Worth covering. MEME is not a definitive verdict on agent memory, but it is a strong benchmark paper with a sharp failure mode: systems can store facts yet fail to maintain dependent state after changes. The review should emphasize the controlled synthetic nature and subset ablations, while treating the Cascade/Absence collapse and the 70x Opus file-memory workaround as the most interesting concrete story.
citations: data/papers/arxiv-2605.12477.md: Abstract and metadata identify the paper, source, 100 controlled episodes, six memory systems, Cascade 3%, Absence 1%, and the claimed 70x cost closure case.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12477.txt:216-218 states the dataset has 100 episodes, two hand-crafted domain graphs, about 35K tokens per episode, and 694 post-change evaluation questions.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12477.txt:275-305 describes the six evaluated systems, in-context baselines, unified LLM roles, GPT-4o judge validation, trivial-pass filtering, and gold-facts ceiling.
data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12477.txt:314-329 reports Table 2 main accuracies: average Cascade 0.03, Absence 0.01, MD-flat 0.42 overall, and Graphiti 0.03 overall.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12477.txt:493-523 summarizes prompt optimization, top-k, answering-LLM, noise, and internal-LLM interventions, with only MD-flat plus Opus 4.7 materially improving Cascade/Absence.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12477.txt:569-579 lists the authors' limitations: hand-crafted graphs, LLM-generated dialogues, 100 episodes, subset ablations, explicit conditional wording, and English-only data.

data/papers/fulltext/arxiv-2605.12477.txt:1646-1649 reports the retrieval-versus-answering breakdown: at larger top-k, many Cascade and most Absence failures occur even when rule and change are retrieved.
